content:
# Exodus Chapter 28 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~49r–50v (PDF pp. 96–99); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 70–71 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_028_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_028_page_002.jpg`, `EXO_028_page_003.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_028_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_028_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Priestly Garments Appointed (vv. 1–5)

**1** "And you [**ve'attah**], bring near [**haqrev**] to you [**eleykha**] Aaron [**et-Aharon**] your brother [**achikha**], and his sons [**ve'et-banav**] with him [**itto**], from the midst of [**mittokh**] the Sons of Israel [**beney Yisra'el**], to minister as priest [**lekhahano**] to Me [**li**]: Aaron [**Aharon**], Nadab [**Nadav**] and Abihu [**va'Avihu**], Eleazar [**El'azar**] and Ithamar [**ve'Itamar**], the sons of [**beney**] Aaron [**Aharon**].

**2** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] garments of [**vigdey**] holiness [**qodesh**] for Aaron [**le'Aharon**] your brother [**achikha**], for glory [**lekhavod**] and for beauty [**uletif'aret**].

**3** And you [**ve'attah**] shall speak [**tedabber**] to [**el**] all [**kol**] the wise [**chakhmey**] of heart [**lev**] whom [**asher**] I have filled [**mille'tiv**] with a spirit of [**ruach**] wisdom [**chokhmah**], and they shall make [**ve'asu**] the garments of [**et-bigdey**] Aaron [**Aharon**] to consecrate him [**leqaddesho**], to minister as priest [**lekhahano**] to Me [**li**].

**4** And these [**ve'elleh**] are the garments [**habbegedim**] which [**asher**] they shall make [**ya'asu**]: a breastpiece [**choshen**] and an ephod [**ve'efod**] and a robe [**ume'il**] and a tunic of [**ukhtonet**] checkered work [**tashbets**] and a turban [**umitsnefet**] and a sash [**ve'avnet**]; and they shall make [**ve'asu**] garments of [**vigdey**] holiness [**qodesh**] for Aaron [**le'Aharon**] your brother [**achikha**] and for his sons [**ulevanav**], to minister as priest [**lekhahano**] to Me [**li**].

**5** And they [**vehem**] shall take [**yiqchu**] the gold [**et-hazzahav**] and the blue [**ve'et-hattekheleth**] and the purple [**ve'et-ha'argaman**] and the scarlet yarn [**ve'et-tola'at hashshani**] and the fine linen [**ve'et-hashshesh**].

---

## The Ephod (vv. 6–14)

**6** And they shall make [**ve'asu**] the ephod [**et-ha'efod**] of gold [**zahav**], blue [**tekheleth**] and purple [**ve'argaman**], scarlet yarn [**tola'at shani**] and fine twisted linen [**veshesh moshzar**], the work of [**ma'aseh**] a skillful craftsman [**choshev**].

**7** Two [**shtey**] shoulder pieces [**khetafot**] joined [**choverot**] shall it have [**yihyeh-lo**] at [**el**] its two [**shney**] ends [**qetsotav**], and it shall be joined [**vechubar**].

**8** And the skillfully woven band [**vecheshev**] of his ephod [**afudato**] which [**asher**] is upon it [**alav**], according to its workmanship [**kema'asehu**], shall be of one piece with it [**mimmenu yihyeh**]: gold [**zahav**], blue [**tekheleth**] and purple [**ve'argaman**] and scarlet yarn [**vetola'at shani**] and fine twisted linen [**veshesh moshzar**].

**9** And you shall take [**velaqachta**] two [**shtey**] onyx [**shoham**] stones [**avney**], and you shall engrave [**ufittachta**] upon them [**alehem**] the names of [**shemot**] the Sons of Israel [**beney Yisra'el**]:

**10** six [**shishah**] of their names [**mishshemotam**] on [**al**] the one [**ha'even**] stone [**ha'echat**], and the names of [**shemot**] the six [**hashshishah**] remaining [**hannotarim**] on [**al**] the second [**ha'even**] stone [**hasshenit**], according to their births [**ketol'dotam**].

**11** With the work of [**ma'aseh**] a stone engraver [**charash even**], like the engravings of [**pittuchey**] a signet [**chotam**], you shall engrave [**tefattach**] the two [**shtey**] stones [**ha'avanim**] with [**al**] the names of [**shemot**] the Sons of Israel [**beney Yisra'el**]; surrounded by [**musabbot**] settings of [**mishbetsot**] gold [**zahav**] you shall make [**ta'aseh**] them [**otam**].

**12** And you shall set [**vesamta**] the two [**shtey**] stones [**ha'avanim**] upon [**al**] the shoulder pieces [**kitfot**] of the ephod [**ha'efod**], stones of [**avney**] remembrance [**zikkaron**] for the Sons of Israel [**livney Yisra'el**]; and Aaron [**Aharon**] shall bear [**venasa**] their names [**et-shemotam**] before [**lifney**] YHWH upon [**al**] his two [**shtey**] shoulders [**khetafav**] as a remembrance [**lezikkaron**].

**13** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] settings of [**mishbetsot**] gold [**zahav**];

**14** and two [**ushtey**] chains of [**sharsherot**] pure [**tahor**] gold [**zahav**]; twisted [**migbalot**] you shall make [**ta'aseh**] them [**otam**], the work of [**ma'aseh**] a rope [**avot**]; and you shall put [**venatattha**] the chains of [**sharsherot**] rope-work [**ha'avotot**] upon [**al**] the settings [**hammishbetsot**].

---

## The Breastpiece of Judgment (vv. 15–30)

**15** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] a breastpiece of [**choshen**] judgment [**mishpat**], the work of [**ma'aseh**] a skillful craftsman [**choshev**]; like the work of [**kema'aseh**] the ephod [**efod**] you shall make it [**ta'asenu**]: of gold [**zahav**], blue [**tekheleth**] and purple [**ve'argaman**] and scarlet yarn [**vetola'at shani**] and fine twisted linen [**veshesh moshzar**] you shall make it [**ta'aseh oto**].

**16** It shall be square [**ravua**], doubled over [**kaful**]; a span [**zeret**] its length [**orkho**] and a span [**zeret**] its width [**rochbo**].

**17** And you shall set in it [**umille'ta vo**] a setting of [**millu'at**] stones [**even**], four [**arba'ah**] rows of [**turim**] stones [**even**]: a row [**tur**] of carnelian [**odem**], topaz [**pitdah**], and emerald [**uvareqet**] — the first [**hatur**] row [**ha'echad**];

**18** and the second [**vehattur**] row [**hasheni**]: turquoise [**nofekh**], sapphire [**sappir**], and diamond [**veyahalom**];

**19** and the third [**vehattur**] row [**hashlishi**]: jacinth [**leshem**], agate [**shevo**], and amethyst [**ve'achlamah**];

**20** and the fourth [**vehattur**] row [**harevi'i**]: beryl [**tarshish**], onyx [**veshoham**], and jasper [**veyashfeh**]; set [**meshubatsim**] in gold [**zahav**] they shall be [**yihyu**] in their settings [**bemillu'otam**].

**21** And the stones [**veha'avanim**] shall be [**tihyeynah**] according to [**al**] the names of [**shemot**] the Sons of Israel [**beney Yisra'el**], twelve [**shteym esreh**], according to [**al**] their names [**shemotam**]; like the engravings of [**pittuchey**] a signet [**chotam**], each [**ish**] according to [**al**] his name [**shemo**] they shall be [**tihyeynah**], for the twelve [**lishtey esreh**] tribes [**shavet**].

**22** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] upon [**al**] the breastpiece [**hachoshen**] chains of [**sharsherot**] twisted [**gavlut**] work [**ma'aseh**], of rope [**avot**] work, of pure [**tahor**] gold [**zahav**].

**23** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] upon [**al**] the breastpiece [**hachoshen**] two [**shtey**] rings of [**tabb'ot**] gold [**zahav**], and you shall put [**venatattha**] the two [**shtey**] rings [**hattabb'ot**] upon [**al**] the two [**shney**] ends of [**qetsot**] the breastpiece [**hachoshen**].

**24** And you shall put [**venatattha**] the two [**shtey**] cords of [**avotot**] gold [**hazzahav**] upon [**al**] the two [**shtey**] rings [**hattabb'ot**] at [**el**] the ends of [**qetsot**] the breastpiece [**hachoshen**].

**25** And the two [**ve'et-shtey**] ends of [**qetsot**] the two [**shtey**] cords [**ha'avotot**] you shall put [**titten**] upon [**al**] the two [**shtey**] settings [**hammishbetsot**], and you shall put [**venatattha**] them upon [**al**] the shoulder pieces of [**kitfot**] the ephod [**ha'efod**], on its front [**el-mul panav**].

**26** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] two [**shtey**] rings of [**tabb'ot**] gold [**zahav**], and you shall set [**vesamta**] them [**otam**] upon [**al**] the two [**shney**] ends of [**qetsot**] the breastpiece [**hachoshen**], upon [**al**] its edge [**sefato**] which [**asher**] is toward [**el-ever**] the ephod [**ha'efod**] inward [**baytah**].

**27** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] two [**shtey**] rings of [**tabb'ot**] gold [**zahav**], and you shall put [**venatattha**] them [**otam**] upon [**al**] the two [**shney**] shoulder pieces of [**kitfot**] the ephod [**ha'efod**] beneath [**millematah**], on [**mimmul**] its front [**panav**], close to [**le'ummat**] its joining [**mechabbarto**], above [**mimma'al**] the skillfully woven band of [**lecheshev**] the ephod [**ha'efod**].

**28** And they shall bind [**veyirkesu**] the breastpiece [**et-hachoshen**] by [**mittabb'otav**] its rings [**el-tabb'ot**] to the rings of [**ha'efod**] the ephod [**bif'til**] with a cord of [**tekheleth**] blue [**lihyot**], to be [**al**] upon [**cheshev**] the skillfully woven band of [**ha'efod**] the ephod [**velo-yizzach**], so that the breastpiece [**hachoshen**] shall not come loose [**me'al**] from upon [**ha'efod**] the ephod.

**29** And Aaron [**venasa Aharon**] shall bear [**et-shemot**] the names of [**beney**] the Sons of Israel [**Yisra'el**] on the breastpiece of [**bechoshen**] judgment [**hammishpat**] upon [**al**] his heart [**libbo**] when he goes in [**bevo'o**] to [**el**] the Holy Place [**haqqodesh**], as a remembrance [**lezikkaron**] before [**lifney**] YHWH continually [**tamid**].

**30** And you shall put [**venatattha**] into [**el**] the breastpiece of [**choshen**] judgment [**hammishpat**] the Urim [**et-ha'Urim**] and the Thummim [**ve'et-haTummim**]; and they shall be [**vehay'u**] upon [**al**] the heart of [**lev**] Aaron [**Aharon**] when he goes in [**bevo'o**] before [**lifney**] YHWH; and Aaron [**venasa Aharon**] shall bear [**et-mishpat**] the judgment of [**beney**] the Sons of Israel [**Yisra'el**] upon [**al**] his heart [**libbo**] before [**lifney**] YHWH continually [**tamid**].

---

## The Robe of the Ephod (vv. 31–35)

**31** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] the robe of [**et-me'il**] the ephod [**ha'efod**] entirely of [**kelil**] blue [**tekheleth**].

**32** And the opening for [**fi**] his head [**rosho**] shall be [**yihyeh**] in the midst of it [**betokhkho**]; a binding [**safah**] shall be [**yihyeh**] for its opening [**lefiv**] all around [**saviv**], the work of [**ma'aseh**] a weaver [**oreg**]; like the opening of [**kefi**] a coat of mail [**tachra**] it shall be [**yihyeh**] for it [**lo**], so that it will not [**lo**] be torn [**yiqqarea**].

**33** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] upon [**al**] its hem [**shulav**] pomegranates of [**rimmoney**] blue [**tekheleth**] and purple [**ve'argaman**] and scarlet yarn [**vetola'at shani**] upon [**al**] its hem [**shulav**] all around [**saviv**], and bells of [**ufa'amoney**] gold [**zahav**] in the midst of them [**betokham**] all around [**saviv**]:

**34** a golden [**zahav**] bell [**pa'amon**] and a pomegranate [**verimon**], a golden [**zahav**] bell [**pa'amon**] and a pomegranate [**verimon**], upon [**al**] the hem of [**shuley**] the robe [**hamme'il**] all around [**saviv**].

**35** And it shall be [**vehayah**] upon [**al**] Aaron [**Aharon**] to minister [**lesharetth**], and its sound [**venishma qolo**] shall be heard [**bevo'o**] when he goes in [**el-haqqodesh**] to the Holy Place [**lifney**] before [**YHWH**] YHWH, and when he goes out [**uvetse'to**], so that he will not [**velo**] die [**yamut**].

---

## The Gold Plate and the Turban (vv. 36–39)

**36** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] a plate [**tsits**] of pure [**tahor**] gold [**zahav**], and you shall engrave [**ufittachta**] upon it [**alav**] the engravings of [**pittuchey**] a signet [**chotam**]: "Holy [**Qodesh**] to YHWH [**laYHWH**]."

**37** And you shall put [**vesamta**] it [**oto**] on [**al**] a cord of [**petil**] blue [**tekheleth**], and it shall be [**vehayah**] on [**al**] the turban [**hammitsnefet**]; on [**el-mul**] the front of [**peney**] the turban [**hammitsnefet**] it shall be [**yihyeh**].

**38** And it shall be [**vehayah**] upon [**al**] the forehead of [**metsach**] Aaron [**Aharon**], and Aaron [**venasa Aharon**] shall bear [**et-avon**] the iniquity [**haqqodashim**] of the holy things [**asher**] which [**yaqddishu**] the Sons of Israel [**beney Yisra'el**] shall consecrate [**lekhol**], for all [**mattenot**] their holy gifts [**qodsheyhem**]; and it shall be [**vehayah**] upon [**al**] his forehead [**mitscho**] continually [**tamid**], for acceptance [**leratson**] for them [**lahem**] before [**lifney**] YHWH.

**39** And you shall weave [**veshibbatsta**] the tunic [**hakketonet**] of fine linen [**shesh**], and you shall make [**ve'asita**] a turban [**mitsnefet**] of fine linen [**shesh**], and a sash [**ve'avnet**] you shall make [**ta'aseh**] the work of [**ma'aseh**] an embroiderer [**roqem**].

---

## Garments for Aaron's Sons (vv. 40–43)

**40** And for the sons of [**livney**] Aaron [**Aharon**] you shall make [**ta'aseh**] tunics [**kuttonot**], and you shall make [**ve'asita**] for them [**lahem**] sashes [**avnetim**], and caps [**umigba'ot**] you shall make [**ta'aseh**] for them [**lahem**], for glory [**lekhavod**] and for beauty [**uletif'aret**].

**41** And you shall clothe [**vehilbashta**] them [**otam**] — Aaron [**et-Aharon**] your brother [**achikha**] and his sons [**ve'et-banav**] with him [**itto**] — and you shall anoint [**umashachta**] them [**otam**] and you shall ordain [**umille'ta**] them [**et-yadam**] and you shall consecrate [**veqiddashta**] them [**otam**], and they shall minister as priests [**vekhihanu**] to Me [**li**].

**42** And you shall make [**va'aseh**] for them [**lahem**] linen [**vad**] breeches [**mikhnesey**] to cover [**lekhassot**] the flesh of [**besar**] nakedness [**ervah**]; from [**mimmothnayim**] the loins [**ve'ad**] to [**yerekayim**] the thighs [**yihyu**] they shall reach.

**43** And they shall be [**vehay'u**] upon [**al**] Aaron [**Aharon**] and upon [**ve'al**] his sons [**banav**] when they go in [**bevo'am**] to [**el**] the Tent of [**ohel**] Meeting [**mo'ed**], or [**o**] when they draw near [**begishtam**] to [**el**] the altar [**hammizbeyach**] to minister [**lesharetth**] in the Holy Place [**baqqodesh**], so that they do not [**velo**] bear [**yis'u**] iniquity [**avon**] and die [**vamethu**]; a statute [**chuqqat**] forever [**olam**] for him [**lo**] and for his seed [**ulzar'o**] after him [**acharav**].

---

## Translator's Notes

### Key Terms

**bigdey qodesh** (vv. 2, 4) -- "garments of holiness" or "holy garments." The construct phrase bigdey qodesh (from b-g-d, "garment, covering" + qodesh, "holiness, separateness") designates the priestly vestments as belonging to the realm of the sacred. The noun beged ordinarily means simply "garment" or "clothing," but when qualified by qodesh it denotes clothing that is ritually set apart, consecrated for exclusive use in divine service. The phrase "for glory and for beauty" (lekhavod uletif'aret) further specifies the purpose of these garments: they confer kavod ("weight, glory, honor") and tif'eret ("beauty, splendor, adornment") upon the wearer. The priestly garments are not merely functional but aesthetic and theological -- they make visible the dignity of the office. The LXX renders bigdey qodesh as stolas hagias ("holy robes"), using stole, which in Greek denotes a long, flowing garment of distinction -- the same term used for the robes of heavenly beings in Revelation 6:11 and 7:9.

**efod** (vv. 6–8) -- "ephod." The noun efod (from '-f-d, "to put on, bind") designates the most distinctive garment of the high priest -- a vest-like outer garment worn over the robe, constructed of the same five materials as the Tabernacle curtains: gold, blue, purple, scarlet yarn, and fine twisted linen. The ephod is described as the "work of a skillful craftsman" (ma'aseh choshev), the same designation applied to the innermost curtains of the Tabernacle (26:1), indicating the highest level of textile artistry. The ephod has two shoulder pieces (khetafot) and a "skillfully woven band" (cheshev) that binds it at the waist. On each shoulder piece is set an onyx stone engraved with the names of six tribes of Israel, so that the high priest literally bears the people on his shoulders before YHWH. The ephod also serves as the attachment point for the breastpiece of judgment. The LXX renders efod as epōmis ("shoulder-garment"), emphasizing its defining structural feature. In later biblical usage, the ephod is associated with inquiring of YHWH (1 Sam 23:9-12; 30:7-8), suggesting a divinatory function connected with the Urim and Thummim housed in the attached breastpiece.

**choshen mishpat** (vv. 15–30) -- "breastpiece of judgment." The noun choshen (etymology uncertain; perhaps from ch-sh-n, "to be beautiful" or related to chotsen, "bosom, lap") denotes the pouch-like garment worn over the high priest's chest, attached to the ephod. The qualifying noun mishpat ("judgment, justice, decision") indicates its function: this is not merely ornamental but judicial -- it is the instrument through which divine judgments are rendered for Israel. The breastpiece is square (ravua), doubled over to form a pouch, one span (approximately 9 inches / 23 cm) in each dimension. Set into its front face are twelve precious stones in four rows of three, each engraved with the name of one of the twelve tribes. Inside the pouch are placed the Urim and Thummim (v. 30), the instruments of divine inquiry. Thus the choshen mishpat concentrates the priestly function of mediation: the high priest bears the names of Israel over his heart and simultaneously carries the means of obtaining divine decisions. The LXX renders choshen as logeion ("oracle, place of words"), interpreting its function rather than its form, and mishpat as kriseōs ("of judgment").

**Urim veTummim** (v. 30) -- "the Urim and the Thummim." The nouns Urim (from '-v-r or '-r-r, perhaps "lights" or "curses") and Tummim (from t-m-m, "completeness, perfection, innocence") designate the sacred lots placed inside the breastpiece of judgment for the purpose of obtaining divine decisions. Their exact nature and mechanism remain uncertain: they may have been two flat objects (stones or pieces of wood) with marked sides that could yield a binary yes/no answer, or they may have operated by some other means. What is theologically clear is their function: they enable the high priest to inquire of YHWH on behalf of Israel and receive authoritative divine responses (Num 27:21; 1 Sam 28:6). The text says nothing about their manufacture -- they are simply "put into" (venatattha el) the breastpiece, suggesting they may have been pre-existing sacred objects not made by human hands, or their origin is deliberately left unexplained. The LXX renders Urim veTummim as dēlōsin kai alētheian ("manifestation and truth"), an interpretive translation that associates the Urim with divine revelation (dēlōsis, "making clear") and the Thummim with divine truth (alētheia). This rendering influenced the later Vulgate's doctrina et veritas ("teaching and truth") and became foundational for understanding the priestly oracle as a medium of truth and revelation.

**me'il ha'efod** (vv. 31–35) -- "the robe of the ephod." The noun me'il (from m-'-l, "to cover, wrap") designates a sleeveless outer garment, longer than the ephod, worn beneath it. The robe of the ephod is distinctive in being made entirely of blue (kelil tekheleth) -- the color associated with heaven and divinity throughout the Tabernacle texts. The robe's hem (shulav) is adorned with alternating pomegranates of colored yarn and golden bells (pa'amonim). The sound of the bells announces the high priest's entry into and departure from the Holy Place; the text explicitly states that this is "so that he will not die" (velo yamut, v. 35). The pomegranate (rimmon) is a pervasive symbol of fertility, abundance, and beauty in the ancient Near East. The LXX renders me'il as hupodutēs ("undergarment"), reflecting its position beneath the ephod. The Vaticanus text preserves the description of the blue robe with its pomegranates (rhoiskou) and bells (kōdōnes).

**tsits** (vv. 36–38) -- "plate, flower, blossom." The noun tsits (from ts-y-ts, "to blossom, shine, gleam") denotes the golden plate affixed to the front of the high priest's turban. The word literally means "blossom" or "flower" (cf. Isa 40:6-8, where tsits refers to the flower of the field that withers), suggesting that the plate was shaped like a blossom or had a gleaming, radiant quality. Engraved upon it in the manner of a signet seal are the words qodesh laYHWH, "Holy to YHWH" -- a declaration that the high priest, and through him the entire sacrificial system, is consecrated to God. The tsits bears the "iniquity of the holy things" (avon haqqodashim, v. 38), meaning it serves an atoning function: even the people's holy offerings may be tainted by inadvertent ritual impurity or error, and the golden plate on Aaron's forehead makes these acceptable (leratson, "for acceptance") before YHWH. The LXX renders tsits as petalon ("leaf, petal, thin plate"), preserving the botanical imagery while clarifying its material form.

### Gemological Notes

**The Twelve Stones** (vv. 17–20) -- The identification of the twelve precious stones set in the breastpiece is among the most debated questions in biblical scholarship, as many of the Hebrew stone names are hapax legomena or near-hapax, and ancient gemological terminology does not map neatly onto modern classifications. The following identifications are traditional and approximate:

- **Row 1:** odem ("redness" -- carnelian/ruby), pitdah (topaz), bareqet ("flashing" -- emerald/beryl)
- **Row 2:** nofekh (turquoise/carbuncle), sappir (lapis lazuli; the modern sapphire is a different stone), yahalom ("striking" -- diamond or onyx)
- **Row 3:** leshem (jacinth/amber), shevo (agate), achlamah (amethyst)
- **Row 4:** tarshish (beryl/chrysolite, perhaps named for the region of Tarshish), shoham (onyx/sardonyx), yashfeh (jasper)

The LXX identifications differ significantly in several cases: sardion (sardius), topazion (topaz), smaragdos (emerald); anthrax (carbuncle), sappheiros (sapphire), iaspis (jasper -- note the displacement from Row 4); ligurion (ligure), achatēs (agate), amethustos (amethyst); chrusolithos (chrysolite), berullion (beryl), onuchion (onyx). The most notable divergence is the LXX's placement of iaspis ("jasper") in the second row where the MT has yahalom, and its rendering of the Row 4 stones in a different sequence. Each stone is engraved with a tribal name, making the breastpiece a lapidary representation of all Israel borne over the high priest's heart. The association of specific stones with specific tribes is not specified in the text and varies in later traditions (cf. the list in Rev 21:19-20, which draws on this passage for the foundations of the New Jerusalem).

### Grammatical Notes

**lekhahano li** (vv. 1, 3, 4, 41) -- "to minister as priest to Me." The Piel infinitive construct of k-h-n ("to serve as priest") with the first person singular indirect object li ("to Me") occurs four times in this chapter, framing the entire discussion of priestly garments with the reminder that priesthood exists for the service of YHWH. The Piel stem here has a denominative function: k-h-n in Piel means "to act as a kohen, to perform priestly duties." The repetition of li ("to Me") anchors the priesthood in divine initiative -- the priests do not serve the people primarily, nor do they serve the sacrificial system; they serve YHWH directly. The priesthood is theocentric, not anthropocentric. The LXX renders lekhahano li as hierateuein moi ("to serve as priest to Me"), using the verb hierateuō from which the English "hieratic" derives.

**ruach chokhmah** (v. 3) -- "spirit of wisdom." The construct phrase ruach chokhmah ("spirit of wisdom") denotes the divine endowment of skill, discernment, and artistry that enables the craftsmen to execute the priestly garments. The word ruach ("spirit, wind, breath") here functions as the animating force behind the craftsmen's ability, and chokhmah ("wisdom") denotes practical skill and expertise, not merely intellectual knowledge. God has "filled" (mille'tiv, Piel of m-l-') the craftsmen with this spirit -- the wisdom is a gift, not an innate talent. This is the same ruach chokhmah that will later be explicitly bestowed upon Bezalel (31:3; 35:31). The LXX renders ruach chokhmah as pneuma aisthēseōs ("spirit of perception/understanding"), using aisthēsis ("sense-perception, discernment") rather than sophia ("wisdom") -- emphasizing aesthetic and practical perception over abstract wisdom.

**mille'ta et-yadam** (v. 41) -- "you shall fill their hand(s)." The phrase mille'ta et-yadam (Piel of m-l-' with the noun yad, "hand") is the Hebrew idiom for priestly ordination -- literally "to fill the hand." The expression likely derives from the practice of placing the first sacrificial portions into the hands of the newly ordained priest (cf. Lev 8:25-28), symbolically filling his hand with the offering he will henceforth present. In English, this is typically rendered "to ordain" or "to consecrate," but the concrete imagery of the Hebrew is theologically significant: the priest's empty hand is filled by God through Moses, indicating that priestly authority is not self-generated but received. The related noun millu'im ("ordination offerings, installation") derives from the same root. The LXX renders this as teleiōseis tas cheiras autōn ("you shall complete/perfect their hands"), using teleioō ("to complete, perfect"), which carries connotations of fulfillment and perfection rather than mere installation.

**velo yis'u avon vamethu** (v. 43) -- "so that they do not bear iniquity and die." The phrase combines the verb nasa ("to bear, carry") with avon ("iniquity, guilt, punishment") and the consequent vamethu ("and they die"). The bearing of avon has a double sense: it can mean bearing guilt (i.e., being held responsible for a transgression) or bearing away guilt (i.e., effecting atonement). Here the context is punitive: failure to wear the prescribed garments when entering the sacred precincts would constitute a capital offense. The juxtaposition of avon and death (mavet) establishes the absolute seriousness of ritual propriety in approaching the holy. The same phrase recurs in Leviticus as a formula for sacral violations (Lev 19:8; 20:17; 22:9). This is not arbitrary ceremonialism but reflects the fundamental biblical principle that the holy God cannot be approached casually or on human terms.

### Structural Notes

Exodus 28 shifts from the construction of the Tabernacle complex (chs. 25-27) to the investiture of its ministers. If the previous chapters answered the question "Where shall YHWH dwell?", this chapter answers "Who shall serve in YHWH's dwelling?" The transition is announced in the final verses of chapter 27, where Aaron and his sons are first mentioned in connection with the lamp-tending, and now the full account of their priestly garments unfolds.

The chapter follows a precise structural logic, moving from the outermost and most visible garment inward. The six garments listed in verse 4 -- breastpiece, ephod, robe, tunic, turban, sash -- are then described in an order that reflects their layering from outside to inside: the ephod (the outermost priestly garment, vv. 6-14), the breastpiece attached to it (vv. 15-30), the blue robe worn beneath the ephod (vv. 31-35), and the turban with its golden plate on the head (vv. 36-38). The tunic and sash receive only brief mention (v. 39) because they are the common priestly garments shared with the ordinary priests, whose simpler vestments are described in verses 40-42.

The chapter's recurring emphasis on "bearing" (nasa) is theologically central. The high priest bears the names of Israel on his shoulders (v. 12), bears the names of Israel over his heart (v. 29), bears the judgment of Israel before YHWH (v. 30), and bears the iniquity of the holy things on his forehead (v. 38). The priestly office is, at its core, a ministry of bearing -- of carrying the people's identity, their need for justice, and their guilt into the presence of God. This fourfold bearing anticipates the Suffering Servant of Isaiah 53 who "bore our griefs" and "carried our sorrows" (Isa 53:4) and whom the New Testament identifies with Christ, the ultimate high priest who "bears the sin of many" (Heb 9:28).

The dual emphases on beauty and holiness in the priestly garments (lekhavod uletif'aret, "for glory and for beauty," v. 2) establish that worship is an aesthetic act as well as a moral one. The God who fills craftsmen with "a spirit of wisdom" (ruach chokhmah, v. 3) to produce these garments values artistry as a form of devotion. The priestly vestments are, in this sense, a theology of beauty: they declare that what is offered to God should be not only ritually correct but visually magnificent.

### Theological Notes

The priestly garments described in Exodus 28 are not merely functional attire but a wearable theology. Each element communicates a facet of the priestly vocation and the relationship between YHWH, the priest, and Israel.

The ephod's onyx stones (vv. 9-12) and the breastpiece's twelve stones (vv. 17-21) together create a system of priestly intercession through presence: the high priest carries all twelve tribes on his person -- on his shoulders (the place of strength and labor) and over his heart (the seat of will, emotion, and thought). The priest does not merely pray for Israel; he embodies Israel before God. This representational theology is the foundation of all subsequent biblical priesthood and reaches its culmination in Christ, who as high priest "always lives to make intercession" (Heb 7:25), bearing His people before the Father not symbolically on stones but actually in His resurrected humanity.

The Urim and Thummim (v. 30) represent YHWH's commitment to guide His people through authoritative decisions. Placed in the breastpiece of "judgment" (mishpat), they ensure that the priest can obtain divine rulings for situations where human wisdom is insufficient. Their placement over the heart -- the organ of decision-making in Hebrew anthropology -- indicates that divine guidance comes not through abstract principles but through the heart of the mediator. The gradual obsolescence of the Urim and Thummim in Israel's history (Ezra 2:63; Neh 7:65) points to their replacement by the prophetic word and ultimately by the indwelling Holy Spirit, who guides believers "into all the truth" (John 16:13).

The golden bells on the robe's hem (vv. 33-35) introduce the theme of priestly vulnerability. The high priest entering the Holy Place stands in a zone of mortal danger -- the text says plainly "so that he will not die" (velo yamut). The bells serve as an audible sign that the priest is alive and performing his duties; their sound simultaneously announces his approach to YHWH and assures those waiting outside that he has not been struck down. The alternation of bells and pomegranates -- sound and silence, declaration and fruitfulness -- creates a liturgical rhythm at the very border between the holy and the most holy.

The inscription qodesh laYHWH on the golden plate (v. 36) is the theological summary of the entire priestly system. Everything the high priest wears, carries, and does is oriented by this declaration: "Holy to YHWH." The plate on the forehead -- the most visible and prominent position on the human body -- proclaims that the priesthood belongs wholly to God. That this same plate bears the iniquity of Israel's holy things (v. 38) reveals the paradox at the heart of biblical priesthood: the one who is "Holy to YHWH" must simultaneously bear the people's unholiness. Holiness and sin converge on the priest's forehead, and it is precisely this convergence that makes the people's offerings acceptable (leratson, "for favor, acceptance") before YHWH. The New Testament sees this fulfilled in Christ, who is both "holy, innocent, undefiled" (Heb 7:26) and the one who "bore our sins in his body on the tree" (1 Pet 2:24).

The concluding command regarding linen breeches (vv. 42-43), though seemingly mundane after the splendor of the preceding garments, establishes the foundational principle of priestly modesty. The covering of "the flesh of nakedness" (besar ervah) recalls the primal shame of Genesis 3:7, 21, where God Himself clothed the first humans after the Fall. The priestly garments, from the glorious ephod to the humble linen breeches, are fundamentally a divine response to human nakedness before the holy. They enable the priest to stand before God without shame -- clothed not in his own righteousness but in garments divinely designed and divinely commanded.

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 28 (pages 70-71) presents the LXX rendering with several notable features and divergences from the MT. Most significantly, the LXX Exodus 28 follows a substantially different verse order than the MT: where the MT presents the ephod first (vv. 6-14), then the breastpiece (vv. 15-30), then the robe (vv. 31-35), and then the turban/plate (vv. 36-38), the LXX interleaves and reorders sections, with the robe and its bells/pomegranates described earlier and some material regarding the ephod's shoulder-stones positioned differently. This reordering may reflect a different Hebrew Vorlage or may represent the LXX translator's attempt to present the garments in their actual order of dressing (inside to outside). At v. 1, the LXX names the four sons -- Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar -- consistent with the MT. At v. 3, the LXX renders ruach chokhmah as pneuma aisthēseōs ("spirit of perception"), a notable interpretive choice. At v. 6, the ephod is rendered as epōmis ("shoulder-garment"). At vv. 17-20, the stone identifications diverge from the MT in several positions (see Gemological Notes above); the Vaticanus manuscript shows the stone names clearly in the three-column layout. At v. 30, the Urim and Thummim are rendered as dēlōsis kai alētheia ("manifestation and truth"), the LXX's most theologically significant interpretive rendering in this chapter. At v. 32, the LXX describes the robe's opening as having a woven border (sumuphes, "woven together"), closely matching the MT's ma'aseh oreg ("work of a weaver"). At v. 36, the inscription on the golden plate is rendered as hagiasma kuriou ("consecration of the Lord"), translating qodesh laYHWH. At v. 41, the LXX renders mille'ta et-yadam as teleiōseis tas cheiras autōn ("you shall perfect their hands"), using teleioō with its rich connotations of completion and perfection. The Vaticanus manuscript shows the chapter continuing from the lower columns of page 70 across the page break onto page 71, with the distinctive chapter division marker (cap. XX -- the LXX chapter/section numbering) visible at the top of page 71.

--- LOGOS
